nativeRange.habitat: Marginal channels, backwaters, swamps, and floodplain lakes with shallow, clear-to-turbid water over mud and clay substrates; areas of dense aquatic vegetation

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, hornwort, water sprite, floating plants) for resting and refuge
broad-leaved plants such as Anubias for mid-water perching
fine sand or smooth gravel substrate (protects barbels)
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin release and hiding
low to moderate flow to replicate still backwater conditions

diet.notes: The Tail Spot Cory is an omnivore with a strong preference for live and frozen zooplankton — Daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), cyclops, and micro-worms are ideal staples. It also accepts small frozen bloodworm, Tubifex, and high-quality nano sinking pellets or crushed flake. Unlike most bottom-feeding corydoras, this species forages partly in the water column, so foods must be small enough for its tiny mouth and ideally offered in suspension or as they sink.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (ember tetras, neon tetras, chili rasboras)
other dwarf Corydoras species (C. pygmaeus, C. habrosus) — confirm compatibility before mixing
small freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) in larger groups
avoidWith: large predatory fish that would eat nano-sized fish
boisterous or fast-moving species that outcompete for food

summary: The Tail Spot Cory (Gastrodermus hastatus) is one of the smallest Corydoras relatives, reaching around 2.5 to 3.5 cm. Distinguished by a bold black spot at the tail base flanked by white dots, it is unusual among corydoras in spending most of its time mid-water, resting on plant leaves rather than the bottom. Groups of eight or more are strongly advised.
careNotes:
The Tail Spot Cory is one of the three recognised dwarf corydoras — alongside C. pygmaeus and C. habrosus — and is arguably the most distinctive in behaviour. Where most corydoras hug the bottom, this species spends the majority of its time in the mid-water column, resting on plant leaves and forming loose shoals among dense vegetation. A group of at least eight individuals is strongly advised; smaller numbers tend to produce timid, stressed fish that hide constantly. A minimum tank footprint of 60 × 30 cm (approximately 40 L) is the practical lower bound, though a 60–80 L planted tank gives a colony room to display natural behaviour.

Water should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15, temperature 20–26 °C). Harder or more alkaline conditions are tolerated short-term but are generally associated with reduced vitality and shorter lifespan. Pristine water quality is important; the Tail Spot Cory is small and sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes. Weekly partial water changes of 20–30% are recommended.

Substrate should be fine sand or smooth gravel to protect the barbels (the small sensory whiskers near the mouth). Dense planting with broad-leaved midground plants such as Anubias or java fern provides essential resting perches off the substrate. Floating plants and tannin-releasing driftwood or leaf litter help recreate the dim, soft-water conditions of its native backwaters.

The three dwarf corydoras species are frequently sold interchangeably as 'dwarf cory' or 'pygmy cory', so it is worth checking markings before purchase. The Tail Spot Cory is identified by a distinctive diamond- or oval-shaped black spot at the base of the tail, flanked by white spots. C. pygmaeus carries a thin horizontal black lateral stripe and is primarily bottom-dwelling. C. habrosus has an irregular horizontal stripe with darker blotches and is the largest of the three at around 3.5 cm. The Tail Spot Cory was reclassified from Corydoras hastatus to Gastrodermus hastatus following a 2024 taxonomic revision; the older name Corydoras hastatus is still encountered in the hobby and in older literature.

The pectoral-fin spines of corydoras can deliver a mildly painful prick when the fish is handled; secretions from the spines are possibly mildly toxic and may irritate skin. Handle with care and never bag the fish in thin plastic alone.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the classic Corydoras T-position. Condition with live or frozen foods (Daphnia, baby brine shrimp) and cool water-change triggers. Females carry 2–4 eggs between cupped pelvic fins during fertilisation before pressing them onto plant leaves or glass. Spawning occurs over 3–4 consecutive days, yielding 30–60 eggs total. Eggs hatch in 3–9 days depending on temperature (faster at the warmer end). Fry are approximately 6 mm at hatching and require infusoria or commercial first-foods for the first week, then progressing to micro-worms and Artemia nauplii. Rated moderate difficulty: the adults spawn willingly but fry rearing demands consistently excellent water quality and very small live foods. Remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras hastatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (25–32 mm SL), temperature 20–26 °C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm, minimum tank footprint 60 × 30 cm, mid-water swim behaviour, diet preference for live zooplankton, breeding (3–4 day spawning, 3–4 day incubation), native range (Amazon basin, Bolivia, Paraguay, Argentina), habitat (marginal channels, backwaters, floodplain lakes), mimetic relationship with sympatric characids, facultative air breathing, and note on mildly toxic pectoral-spine secretions. Care level: moderate (fry-rearing challenging).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras hastatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), maximum size (2.4 cm SL — conservative; SeriouslyFish reports 32 mm/3.2 cm SL; 3.5 cm TL used as upper adult bound including caudal fin), temperature 25–28 °C (slightly warmer than SeriouslyFish 20–26 °C — SeriouslyFish range adopted as more conservative for captive conditions), pH 6.0–8.0 (narrowed to 7.5 upper limit per SeriouslyFish recommendation), native range (Amazon and Paraguay River basins, South America), conservation status Least Concern, facultative air-breathing confirmed. Divergence: FishBase temperature max 28 °C vs SeriouslyFish 26 °C — used SeriouslyFish cap of 26 °C as safer aquarium target.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Pygmy Corydoras
additional.notes: Species identification guide and trade-level distinctions: C. hastatus = large black caudal-peduncle spot flanked by white dots; C. pygmaeus = thin horizontal black lateral stripe; C. habrosus = irregular horizontal stripe with blotches. Temperature 22–26 °C and pH 6.4–7.5 consistent with SeriouslyFish. Tank size: 10+ gallons (38+ L) for a group; 40 L adopted here as practical minimum. Diet: frozen daphnia, cyclops, baby brine shrimp, nano pellets. Breeding: females deposit single eggs on plants; hatch 2–5 days. Article focuses primarily on C. pygmaeus but explicitly covers all three dwarf species for identification.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning follows the T-position pattern typical of corydoras and takes place over 3–4 days, producing 30–60 eggs deposited on plant leaves or the aquarium glass. Incubation lasts 3–9 days depending on temperature. Fry rearing is the most demanding stage, requiring infusoria or commercial first-foods and consistently excellent water quality. Adults should be removed after spawning to prevent egg predation.
